title: Fourier Series and Partial Differential Equations
subtitle: From Fourier Series to Separation of Variables and the Classical PDEs
description: A Fourier series rebuilds any reasonable periodic function as an infinite superposition of sines and cosines, f(x)=a_0/2+sum a_n cos(n pi x/L)+b_n sin(n pi x/L), with each coefficient extracted by a single orthogonality integral (the Euler-Fourier formulas). Even functions collapse to a pure cosine series and odd functions to a pure sine series, and a function defined only on [0,L] can be forced into either by an even or odd extension -- the half-range expansions. This machinery is exactly what unlocks partial differential equations. Separation of variables u(x,t)=X(x)T(t) splits a linear PDE with homogeneous boundary conditions into two ODEs joined by a separation constant, turning the spatial part into the eigenvalue BVP of Unit 17; the boundary data selects the eigenfunctions and the initial data is matched by a Fourier series. The Laplacian nabla^2 u = u_xx + u_yy measures how a value differs from its neighborhood average and is the spatial heart of the three classical equations: the heat equation u_t = alpha^2 u_xx (diffusion, parabolic), the wave equation u_tt = c^2 u_xx (vibration, hyperbolic, with d'Alembert's traveling-wave solution), and Laplace's equation nabla^2 u = 0 (steady state, elliptic, harmonic functions).

atAGlance.title: Unit 18 at a Glance: Fourier Series as the Key to Partial Differential Equations
atAGlance.summary: A \highlight{Fourier series} expresses a periodic function as a sum of sines and cosines, and because the trigonometric system is \emph{orthogonal}, every coefficient is one independent integral --- the \highlight{Euler--Fourier formulas}. Symmetry simplifies the work: \highlight{even} functions need only cosines, \highlight{odd} functions only sines, and a function on a half-interval can be expanded as either via a half-range extension. This is the toolkit that cracks \highlight{partial differential equations}: \highlight{separation of variables} $u=X(x)T(t)$ splits a PDE into ODEs linked by a separation constant, the boundary conditions reproduce the eigenvalue BVP of Unit~17, and the initial profile is matched by a Fourier series. The \highlight{Laplacian} $\nabla^2 u$ governs the three classical equations --- heat (diffusion), wave (vibration), and Laplace (steady state).

bullets.body: any reasonable periodic signal is a superposition of pure frequencies; the Fourier transform is the continuous-spectrum limit of the Fourier series.
$a_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$, $b_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$, derived from trig orthogonality.
even $\Rightarrow$ cosine series, odd $\Rightarrow$ sine series; extend a function on $0\le x\le L$ evenly or oddly to get either.
$u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)$ turns a linear homogeneous PDE into two ODEs; the spatial ODE is the Unit~17 eigenvalue problem.

bullets.lead: The Laplacian and the classical PDEs
bullets.body: $\nabla^2 u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}$ drives heat $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$, wave $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$, and Laplace $\nabla^2 u=0$.

modules.title: Fourier Series and Transform Intuition
modules.topics: A periodic function as a superposition of pure-frequency sines and cosines; the time-domain versus frequency-domain picture; harmonics and the fundamental frequency; the Fourier transform as the continuous-spectrum limit of the Fourier series.

formulas.title: The Fourier Series --- Superposition of Harmonics
Harmonics and the Fundamental Frequency
formulas.body: \begin{gathered} \text{the } n\text{th harmonic has frequency } \tfrac{n}{2L}; \\ n=1 \text{ is the \emph{fundamental}},\ n\ge 2 \text{ the \emph{overtones}.} \end{gathered}

formulas.title: The Fourier Transform --- Continuous Spectrum
formulas.body: \begin{gathered} \hat f(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,e^{-2\pi i \xi x}\,dx; \\ \text{the } L\to\infty \text{ limit turns the discrete harmonics } n/2L \text{ into a continuum } \xi. \end{gathered}

methodology.title: Method --- Read a Function in the Frequency Domain
steps: Treat the signal $f(x)$ as living in the \emph{time} (or space) domain --- its value at each point.
Ask which pure frequencies $\cos(n\pi x/L),\ \sin(n\pi x/L)$ it is built from, and with what amplitudes $a_n,b_n$.
Plot the amplitudes against frequency: this \emph{frequency-domain} spectrum is an equivalent description of the same signal.
For a non-periodic signal, let the period $2L\to\infty$; the discrete spectrum fills in to a continuous one and the sum becomes the Fourier transform integral.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Spectrum of a Two-Tone Signal
examples.setup: Identify the Fourier content of $f(x)=3+5\cos(\pi x)-2\sin(3\pi x)$ on the interval of period $2$ (so $L=1$).
steps: \strong{Match the template: } f(x)=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_n\big(a_n\cos n\pi x+b_n\sin n\pi x\big) \text{ with } L=1.
\strong{Constant term: } \frac{a_0}{2}=3 \Rightarrow a_0=6 \text{ (the average / DC level).}
\strong{Cosine amplitude: } a_1=5 \text{ (a fundamental cosine at frequency } \tfrac12).
\strong{Sine amplitude: } b_3=-2 \text{ (a third-harmonic sine); all other } a_n,b_n=0.
\strong{Spectrum: } \text{only three lines --- DC, } n=1 \text{ cosine, } n=3 \text{ sine --- because } f \text{ is already a finite trig sum.}

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: A Fourier series says any reasonable periodic function is a \highlight{superposition of pure frequencies} --- a chord of sines and cosines. The same signal then has two equivalent portraits: the familiar \highlight{time-domain} graph and the \highlight{frequency-domain} spectrum of amplitudes. Letting the period grow without bound turns the discrete spectrum into a continuous one, and the series becomes the Fourier transform.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: The constant term is $a_0/2$, \warn{not} $a_0$ --- the factor of $\tfrac12$ is what makes the single Euler--Fourier formula $a_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$ work for $n=0$ as well. Also, a Fourier \emph{series} needs a \warn{periodic} (or finite-interval) function; a non-periodic signal on the whole line requires the Fourier \emph{transform}, not a series.

modules.title: Computing Fourier Series
modules.topics: The Euler--Fourier coefficient formulas on $-L\le x\le L$; orthogonality of the trigonometric system as their engine; the constant (DC) term $a_0/2$; convergence at jumps to the midpoint of the one-sided limits (Dirichlet conditions).

formulas.title: The Euler--Fourier Coefficient Formulas
formulas.body: a_n = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L}^{L} f(x)\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \ (n\ge 0), \qquad b_n = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L}^{L} f(x)\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \ (n\ge 1).

formulas.title: Orthogonality of the Trigonometric System
formulas.body: \int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\cos\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx = L\,\delta_{nm}, \quad \int_{-L}^{L}\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx = L\,\delta_{nm}, \quad \int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx = 0.

formulas.title: Convergence at a Jump (Dirichlet)
formulas.body: \text{At a jump } x_0,\ \text{the series converges to } \tfrac12\big[f(x_0^-)+f(x_0^+)\big], \text{ the midpoint of the one-sided limits.}

methodology.title: Method --- Compute a Fourier Series
steps: Identify the half-period $L$ so the function repeats with period $2L$, and write the target $f=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_n(a_n\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}+b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L})$.
Compute $a_0=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\,dx$ --- twice the average value of $f$ over one period.
Compute $a_n$ and $b_n$ from the Euler--Fourier integrals, using integration by parts where $f$ is polynomial times trig.
Exploit symmetry to skip half the work: if $f$ is even all $b_n=0$; if $f$ is odd all $a_n=0$ (and $a_0=0$).
State the series and note its value at any jump is the midpoint $\tfrac12[f(x_0^-)+f(x_0^+)]$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Fourier Series of $f(x)=x$ on $-\pi\le x\le \pi$
examples.setup: Find the Fourier series of the $2\pi$-periodic extension of $f(x)=x$ on $-\pi\le x\le\pi$ (so $L=\pi$), computing every coefficient in full.
steps: \strong{Symmetry: } f(x)=x \text{ is odd} \Rightarrow a_0=0 \text{ and } a_n=0 \text{ for all } n; \text{ only the } b_n \text{ survive.}
\strong{Set up } b_n: \ b_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} x\sin(nx)\,dx=\frac2\pi\int_{0}^{\pi} x\sin(nx)\,dx \ \text{(even integrand).}
\strong{By parts: } u=x,\ dv=\sin(nx)\,dx \Rightarrow du=dx,\ v=-\tfrac1n\cos(nx).
\int_0^\pi x\sin(nx)\,dx=\Big[-\tfrac{x}{n}\cos(nx)\Big]_0^\pi+\tfrac1n\int_0^\pi\cos(nx)\,dx=-\tfrac{\pi}{n}\cos(n\pi)+\tfrac1{n^2}\big[\sin(nx)\big]_0^\pi.
\strong{Evaluate: } \cos(n\pi)=(-1)^n,\ \sin(n\pi)=0 \Rightarrow \int_0^\pi x\sin(nx)\,dx=-\tfrac{\pi}{n}(-1)^n=(-1)^{n+1}\tfrac{\pi}{n}.
\strong{Coefficient: } b_n=\frac2\pi\cdot(-1)^{n+1}\tfrac{\pi}{n}=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2}{n}.
\strong{Series: } x=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2}{n}\sin(nx)=2\Big(\sin x-\tfrac12\sin 2x+\tfrac13\sin 3x-\cdots\Big),\ \text{converging to } 0 \text{ at } x=\pm\pi.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: The Euler--Fourier formulas are pure \highlight{orthogonality}: integrating $f$ against a single basis wave annihilates every other term and leaves one coefficient. The constant $a_0/2$ is the function's \highlight{average}; the $a_n,b_n$ are how strongly each harmonic is present. Symmetry is the great labor-saver --- recognizing $f$ as even or odd erases half the integrals before you compute anything.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: Watch the normalizations: the coefficients use $\frac1L$ (over the full period $2L$), while the half-range formulas of the next module use $\frac2L$ (over $0\le x\le L$). \warn{Mixing the two} is the most common Fourier error. And the series equals $f$ only where $f$ is continuous --- at a \warn{jump} it converges to the midpoint $\tfrac12[f(x_0^-)+f(x_0^+)]$, not to either one-sided value.

modules.title: Fourier Series for Differential Equations
modules.topics: Even and odd functions and their product rules; the pure cosine series (even) and pure sine series (odd); half-range expansions via even or odd extension of a function given only on $0\le x\le L$; choosing the expansion that matches the boundary conditions of a PDE.

formulas.title: Even/Odd Symmetry Collapses the Series
formulas.body: f \text{ even} \Rightarrow b_n=0 \ (\text{pure cosine series}); \qquad f \text{ odd} \Rightarrow a_n=0 \ (\text{pure sine series}, \text{ and } a_0=0).

formulas.title: Half-Range Cosine Series (Even Extension)
formulas.body: f(x)=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}, \qquad a_n=\frac{2}{L}\int_0^L f(x)\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \ \ (n\ge 0).

formulas.title: Half-Range Sine Series (Odd Extension)
formulas.body: f(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}, \qquad b_n=\frac{2}{L}\int_0^L f(x)\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.

methodology.title: Method --- Build a Half-Range Expansion
steps: You are given $f$ only on $0\le x\le L$ and must represent it by sines \emph{or} cosines alone.
For a \emph{sine} series, extend $f$ as an \emph{odd} function to $-L\le x\le 0$; for a \emph{cosine} series, extend it as an \emph{even} function.
Apply the half-range formula with the $\frac2L$ normalization (the $-L$ to $0$ contribution doubles the $0$ to $L$ integral).
Let the boundary conditions of the target PDE pick the series: Dirichlet ends ($u=0$) call for the \emph{sine} series, Neumann ends ($u_x=0$) for the \emph{cosine} series.
Write the expansion, remembering the odd extension forces $f(0)=f(L)=0$ in the limit, matching homogeneous Dirichlet data.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Half-Range Sine Series of $f(x)=1$ on $0\le x\le L$
examples.setup: Expand the constant $f(x)=1$ on $0\le x\le L$ in a half-range \emph{sine} series (odd extension), the form needed for a Dirichlet BVP.
steps: \strong{Formula: } b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L 1\cdot\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.
\strong{Integrate: } \int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\Big[-\frac{L}{n\pi}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big]_0^L=-\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(\cos n\pi-1\big).
\strong{Simplify: } \cos n\pi=(-1)^n \Rightarrow \int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big).
\strong{Coefficient: } b_n=\frac2L\cdot\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{2}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{4}{n\pi}, & n \text{ odd},\\[4pt] 0, & n \text{ even}.\end{cases}
\strong{Series: } 1=\frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}=\frac4\pi\Big(\sin\frac{\pi x}{L}+\tfrac13\sin\frac{3\pi x}{L}+\cdots\Big),\ 0<x<L.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: Symmetry is a choice you can \highlight{impose}. A function living only on $0\le x\le L$ has no symmetry of its own, but you may extend it \highlight{evenly} to get a cosine series or \highlight{oddly} to get a sine series. Which you pick is dictated by the PDE: \highlight{Dirichlet} boundary data wants the \highlight{sine} series (it vanishes at the ends), \highlight{Neumann} data wants the \highlight{cosine} series (its slope vanishes).

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: The half-range formulas carry $\frac2L$, \warn{not} $\frac1L$ --- the odd or even extension doubles the integral over $0\le x\le L$. Do not also integrate over $-L$ to $L$; that double-counts. And the sine series of a function with $f(0)\neq 0$ (like $f=1$ above) \warn{converges to $0$ at the endpoints} because the odd extension forces a jump there --- expected, not an error.

modules.title: Separable Partial Differential Equations
modules.topics: The separation-of-variables ansatz $u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)$; splitting a linear homogeneous PDE into two ODEs joined by a separation constant; the role of homogeneous boundary conditions in producing the spatial eigenvalue BVP; the classical PDEs and their boundary/initial data.

formulas.title: The Separation Ansatz and Constant
formulas.body: \begin{gathered} u(x,t)=X(x)\,T(t); \\ \frac{1}{\alpha^2}\frac{T'}{T}=\frac{X''}{X}=-\lambda \ \ (\text{constant: each side depends on a different variable}). \end{gathered}

formulas.title: The Two Resulting ODEs (Heat Equation)
formulas.body: X''+\lambda X=0 \ \ (\text{spatial, an eigenvalue BVP}); \qquad T'+\alpha^2\lambda T=0 \ \ (\text{temporal, first order, } T_n=e^{-\alpha^2\lambda_n t}).

formulas.title: Superposition of the Product Solutions
formulas.body: \begin{gathered} u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\,X_n(x)\,T_n(t); \\ \text{the } b_n \text{ are fixed by matching the initial condition with a Fourier series.} \end{gathered}

methodology.title: Method --- Solve a PDE by Separation of Variables
steps: Assume a product solution $u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)$ and substitute into the PDE.
Divide through so all $x$-dependence is on one side and all $t$-dependence on the other; set both equal to the separation constant $-\lambda$.
Solve the \emph{spatial} ODE with the homogeneous boundary conditions first --- this is the Unit~17 eigenvalue BVP, yielding eigenvalues $\lambda_n$ and eigenfunctions $X_n$.
Solve the \emph{temporal} ODE for each $\lambda_n$ to get $T_n(t)$, then form the superposition $u=\sum_n b_n X_n T_n$.
Impose the initial condition $u(x,0)=f(x)$ and read off the $b_n$ as the Fourier (half-range) coefficients of $f$.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Separating the Heat Equation
examples.setup: Separate $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ on $0\le x\le L$ with $u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$, and identify the two ODEs and the spatial eigenpairs.
steps: \strong{Substitute } u=X(x)T(t):\ XT'=\alpha^2 X''T.
\strong{Divide by } \alpha^2 XT:\ \frac{T'}{\alpha^2 T}=\frac{X''}{X}.
\strong{Separation constant: } \text{LHS depends only on } t, \text{ RHS only on } x \Rightarrow \text{both} =-\lambda.
\strong{Spatial BVP: } X''+\lambda X=0,\ X(0)=X(L)=0 \Rightarrow \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\ X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L} \ (\text{Unit 17}).
\strong{Temporal ODE: } T'+\alpha^2\lambda_n T=0 \Rightarrow T_n(t)=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}.
\strong{Product modes: } u_n(x,t)=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t},\ \text{ready to superpose.}

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: Separation of variables is the master move for linear PDEs: assuming $u=X(x)T(t)$ \highlight{decouples} the equation into two ODEs tied by a single separation constant. The homogeneous boundary conditions turn the spatial ODE into the \highlight{eigenvalue BVP of Unit~17}, so its eigenfunctions become the building blocks; \highlight{superposition} assembles them and the \highlight{initial condition} fixes the weights through a Fourier series.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: Separation of variables needs the PDE to be \warn{linear and homogeneous with homogeneous boundary conditions} --- a nonzero boundary value or a forcing term breaks the clean split (handle those by first subtracting a steady-state/particular solution). Also, always solve the \warn{spatial} eigenvalue problem before the temporal one: the boundary conditions live in $x$, and they are what quantize $\lambda$.

modules.title: The Laplacian
modules.topics:
The Laplacian $
abla^2 u=
abla\cdot
abla u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}$ as the divergence of the gradient; its meaning as how a value departs from its local average; Laplace's equation $
abla^2 u=0$ and harmonic functions; the steady-state limit of diffusion; polar/spherical forms.

formulas.title: The Laplacian --- Divergence of the Gradient
formulas.body:
abla^2 u = 
abla\cdot(
abla u) =  rac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}+ rac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}+ rac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \ \ (	ext{Cartesian, here through } 3	ext{D}).

formulas.title: Laplace's Equation and Harmonic Functions
formulas.body: \begin{gathered} \nabla^2 u = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad u \text{ is \emph{harmonic}}; \\ \text{value at a point} = \text{average over any surrounding circle/sphere (mean value).} \end{gathered}

formulas.title: The Laplacian in Polar Coordinates
formulas.body: \nabla^2 u = u_{rr} + \frac{1}{r}u_r + \frac{1}{r^2}u_{\theta\theta} \ \ (\text{the natural form for disks and annuli}).

methodology.title: Method --- Test and Interpret the Laplacian
steps: Compute the second partial in each spatial variable and add them to form $\nabla^2 u$.
Read the sign as curvature versus the neighborhood: $\nabla^2 u>0$ means the value sits \emph{below} its local average (a dip), $\nabla^2 u<0$ means \emph{above} (a bump).
If $\nabla^2 u=0$ everywhere, the function is harmonic --- it equals its local average and has no interior maxima or minima (maximum principle).
For diffusion, recognize $\nabla^2 u$ as the spatial driver: heat flows so as to erase departures from the average, and the steady state ($u_t=0$) is exactly Laplace's equation.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Verify a Harmonic Function
examples.setup: Show that $u(x,y)=x^2-y^2$ is harmonic, and contrast it with $v(x,y)=x^2+y^2$.
steps: \strong{Gradient pieces: } u_x=2x,\ u_{xx}=2; \qquad u_y=-2y,\ u_{yy}=-2.
\strong{Laplacian of } u: \ \nabla^2 u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}=2+(-2)=0 \Rightarrow u \text{ is harmonic.}
\strong{Now } v: \ v_{xx}=2,\ v_{yy}=2 \Rightarrow \nabla^2 v=4\neq 0 \Rightarrow v \text{ is \emph{not} harmonic.}
\strong{Interpretation: } \nabla^2 v=4>0 \Rightarrow v \text{ sits below its local average --- a bowl with an interior minimum.}
\strong{Contrast: } u=x^2-y^2 \text{ is a saddle; its curvatures cancel, so no interior extremum (maximum principle).}

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: The Laplacian $\nabla^2 u$ is the \highlight{divergence of the gradient} --- a scalar measuring how a point's value departs from the \highlight{average of its neighbors}. Where it is positive the point is a relative dip, where negative a relative bump, and where it is \highlight{zero the function is harmonic} and equals its local average. This single operator is the spatial engine of all three classical PDEs.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: The Laplacian is a \emph{sum} of unmixed second partials, $u_{xx}+u_{yy}$ --- \warn{do not include the mixed partial} $u_{xy}$, and do not confuse $\nabla^2 u$ (a scalar) with the gradient $\nabla u$ (a vector) or the Hessian (a matrix). In curved coordinates you \warn{cannot} just sum naive second derivatives: polar form carries the extra $\frac1r u_r$ and $\frac1{r^2}u_{\theta\theta}$ terms.

modules.title: The Heat and Wave Equations
modules.topics: The heat equation $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ (parabolic diffusion) solved by separation plus a Fourier sine series; the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ (hyperbolic vibration) with standing-mode and d'Alembert traveling-wave solutions; Duhamel's principle for a forced wave; the classification of the three classical equations.

formulas.title: Heat Equation --- Fourier Solution
formulas.body: u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}, \qquad b_n=\frac{2}{L}\int_0^L f(x)\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.

methodology.title: Method --- Solve the 1D Heat Equation on a Rod
steps: Separate $u=X(x)T(t)$ and solve the spatial BVP $X''+\lambda X=0$, $X(0)=X(L)=0$ to get $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$, $X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$.
Solve $T'+\alpha^2\lambda_n T=0$ for $T_n=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}$, so each mode decays faster the higher its frequency.
Superpose: $u(x,t)=\sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}$.
Set $t=0$: $u(x,0)=\sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}=f(x)$, so the $b_n$ are the half-range \emph{sine} coefficients of the initial temperature $f$.
Evaluate $b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L f\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$ and write the full series solution.

examples.title: Worked Example --- Heat on a Rod with Initial Temperature $f(x)=100$
examples.setup: A rod $0\le x\le L$ has ends held at $0^\circ$ ($u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$) and uniform initial temperature $u(x,0)=100$. Solve $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ completely, computing the coefficients in full.
steps: \strong{Separate: } u=X T \Rightarrow X''+\lambda X=0,\ X(0)=X(L)=0,\ \text{ and } T'+\alpha^2\lambda T=0.
\strong{Spatial eigenpairs: } \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\quad X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L},\quad n=1,2,3,\dots
\strong{Temporal factor: } T_n(t)=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}.
\strong{Superposition: } u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}.
\strong{Match } t=0: \ \sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}=100 \Rightarrow b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L 100\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.
\strong{Integrate: } \int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big) \Rightarrow b_n=\frac{200}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big).
\strong{Odd-only: } b_n=\dfrac{400}{n\pi}\ (n \text{ odd}),\ 0\ (n \text{ even}).
\strong{Solution: } u(x,t)=\frac{400}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2((2k+1)\pi/L)^2 t}\ \xrightarrow{t\to\infty}\ 0.

concept.title: Key Takeaway
concept.body: The classical PDEs all yield to the same pipeline: \highlight{separate}, solve the \highlight{spatial eigenvalue BVP}, attach the time factor, \highlight{superpose}, and fit the initial data with a \highlight{Fourier series}. Their characters differ --- the \highlight{heat} equation smooths and decays (high modes die fastest), the \highlight{wave} equation oscillates and propagates (d'Alembert's two traveling waves), and \highlight{Laplace} gives the steady state --- but Fourier analysis is the common engine.

warning.title: Common Pitfall
warning.body: Match the time factor to the equation's order in $t$: the heat equation is \emph{first} order so each mode carries a single decaying $e^{-\alpha^2\lambda_n t}$, while the wave equation is \emph{second} order so each mode needs \warn{two} time functions $\cos(c\sqrt{\lambda_n}\,t)$ and $\sin(c\sqrt{\lambda_n}\,t)$ --- and therefore \emph{two} initial conditions (shape \emph{and} velocity). Using a single exponential for the wave equation \warn{loses half the solution}.

cheat.subtitle: Condensed Cheat Sheet: Fourier Coefficients $a_n,b_n$, Half-Range Series, Separation of Variables, the Laplacian $\nabla^2 u$, and the Heat/Wave Equations

takeaway.title: Unit 18 Cheat-Sheet Takeaway
takeaway.body: Fourier analysis is the through-line. A periodic function is a sum of harmonics with coefficients $a_n,b_n$ pulled out by \highlight{orthogonality}; \highlight{even/odd symmetry} reduces it to a pure cosine or sine series, and half-range extensions let you choose. That same series solves PDEs: \highlight{separation of variables} $u=X(x)T(t)$ splits the equation into ODEs, the spatial part is the Unit~17 \highlight{eigenvalue BVP}, and the initial data is matched by a Fourier series. The \highlight{Laplacian} $\nabla^2 u$ powers the heat ($u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$), wave ($u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$), and Laplace ($\nabla^2 u=0$) equations.
